sections.heading: Devotion and Its Call (Part I)
sections.html:
The Universal Call to Devotion

Francis de Sales addressed the Introduction to the Devout Life originally to a single layperson — a noblewoman named Louise de Chastel, whose spiritual direction he had been conducting through letters. He gave her the pen-name Philothea: soul devoted to God. The choice of audience and name encapsulate the book's revolutionary thesis: true devotion is not the exclusive property of monks, priests, and religious women, but the calling and the possibility of every baptized Christian in every station of life.

This was not a trivial claim in early seventeenth-century France. Received wisdom held that the contemplative life — the life of prayer, virtue, and spiritual depth — was available in its fullness only to those who had withdrawn from the world into the cloister. The spirituality available to lay people, to married persons, to those engaged in commerce or governance or the management of households, was of a lesser and more rudimentary kind. Francis rejected this framework entirely. Devotion, he insists, far from being incompatible with the state of the married person, the merchant, the soldier, or the courtier, is in fact fitted to each and perfects each. A soldier who is devout fights more justly; a merchant who is devout trades more honestly; a mother who is devout raises children more wisely and loves her husband more purely.

“But godliness with contentment is great gain.” (1 Timothy 6:6)

Francis grounds his vision in what we might call a theology of the particular vocation. Paul's principle in 1 Corinthians 7:17 — “let every man abide in the same calling wherein he was called” — is not a counsel of spiritual mediocrity but a recognition that God sanctifies each person within the concrete particularity of their life. The devotion of a bishop should look like the devotion of a bishop; the devotion of a tradesman should look like the devotion of a tradesman. The error is to imagine that devotion for the layperson must look like the devotion of a Carthusian — and then to give up on devotion when one cannot achieve that.

The General Confession and the Turning of the Will

The first part of the Introduction guides Philothea through what Francis calls the preparation for devotion: a general confession of the whole of one's past life (made with sincerity and without excessive anxiety) followed by a formal act of self-consecration to God. This is not a second conversion in any sacramental sense but a deliberate and explicit turning of the will — a moment of clarity in which the soul acknowledges where it has been and declares where it intends to go.

Francis makes a distinction that is practically important and often overlooked: the difference between the commission of sins, the retention of attachment to sin, and the persistence of affection for sinful things. A person may genuinely repent of past sins and yet retain a disordered inclination toward the kinds of things those sins involved. And a person may retain inclinations and yet still nurse a secret affection for the sin itself — a reluctance to give it up entirely, a wish that it were not sinful. Francis insists that genuine devotion requires not only the renunciation of sin but also, progressively, the renunciation of attachment and affection. This is the long work of purification that the rest of the book addresses.

The foundation of devotion, Francis consistently emphasizes, is love. Not fear, not duty, not discipline alone — though these have their place — but love of God and of the neighbor whom God loves. All the practices and exercises he commends are in service of this love; they are means, not ends. A person who becomes more disciplined and regular in prayer but less loving has missed the point. A person who becomes warmer and more genuinely charitable, even if their prayer is irregular, is moving in the right direction.

  • 1 Corinthians 7:17 — every person called to devotion in their own station
  • Matthew 5:3–12 — the Beatitudes as the charter of devotion for all
  • John 15:5 — “without me ye can do nothing” — the ground of all spiritual effort

sections.heading: Prayer and the Sacraments (Parts II–III)
sections.html:
Mental Prayer and Meditation

The heart of Francis's practical spirituality is mental prayer — what he also calls meditation. He devotes careful attention to its method: one places oneself in the presence of God, applies the imagination to a scene or truth (the life of Christ, the four last things, a virtue, a mystery of faith), allows the affections to be moved by what the imagination presents, forms practical resolutions, and closes with thanksgiving and a gathering of what has been received into a spiritual nosegay to carry through the day.

Francis includes in Part I a series of model meditations that are themselves works of spiritual literature: meditations on creation, on God's gifts to the soul, on sin, on death, on judgment, on hell, and on heaven. The meditation on death is particularly striking — Francis asks Philothea to imagine herself on her deathbed, looking back on the life she is now living: What will she wish she had done? What will seem important and what trivial? This exercise in the memento mori tradition is not meant to produce despair but reorientation — to help the soul distinguish what genuinely matters from what merely occupies.

“I have learned, in whatsoever state I am, therewith to be content.” (Philippians 4:11)

Paul's contentment (autarkeia) is, for Francis, one of the signatures of genuine devotion: the soul that has found God in its particular state is no longer driven by restlessness, comparison, or the fantasy that spiritual life would be easier in different circumstances. The practice of mental prayer is the principal means by which this contentment is cultivated — not by suppressing desire but by redirecting it toward the One who can fulfill it.

The Eucharist, Confession, and Spiritual Friendship

Francis describes the Eucharist as the sun of the spiritual life, around which all other devotional practices orbit. He urges frequent communion — a practice that was somewhat controversial in his day, when many held that infrequency showed greater reverence — on the grounds that the soul needs nourishment and that the reverence owed to Christ in the Eucharist is better shown by frequent, humble reception than by rare, proud abstinence. The soul that says it is unworthy to communicate frequently is, Francis gently suggests, perhaps more attached to its sense of its own unworthiness than to Christ himself.

Confession, for Francis, is not merely the sacrament of absolution but an occasion for spiritual direction and growth. He recommends that Philothea confess regularly, with sincerity and without excessive scrupulosity, and that she seek out a confessor who is both learned and devout — not merely one or the other. A learned confessor who lacks devotion will give correct but cold direction; a devout confessor who lacks learning may give warm but mistaken guidance. The combination of wisdom and holiness is rare and to be sought with prayer.

One of the most celebrated sections of the Introduction is Francis's treatment of friendship. He distinguishes sharply between vain and frivolous friendship (based on mutual pleasure or worldly advantage), true natural friendship (based on shared virtue and honest affection), and holy friendship — friendship ordered toward God and rooted in shared love of Christ. He cites the relationship of David and Jonathan, of Ruth and Naomi, as examples of friendship that is itself a path of sanctification. “Love everyone with the love of charity,” he advises, “but form friendships only with those with whom you can exchange virtue” — for we inevitably become like those with whom we spend our deepest affection.

  • John 15:5 — union with Christ as the source of all spiritual fruit
  • Philippians 4:11 — contentment learned through practice
  • Matthew 5:8 — “Blessed are the pure in heart: for they shall see God”

sections.heading: Virtue and Perseverance (Parts IV–V)
sections.html:
The Little Virtues and Devotion in One's Vocation

The fourth part of the Introduction is Francis's most practical and, in some ways, most original contribution to the spiritual tradition. He commends what he calls the little virtues — humility, gentleness, patience, simplicity, modesty — as the proper path of ordinary Christian sanctity. This is a deliberate and even polemical choice. The Christian tradition has often glorified the heroic virtues — the martyrdom, the dramatic conversion, the extraordinary penitential labors of the desert fathers. Francis does not deny the value of these, but he insists that for most Christians in most circumstances, the path of sanctification runs through the small daily decisions: how we respond to a slight, how patiently we bear inconvenience, how honestly we speak, how gently we treat those in our power.

Francis is particularly searching on the subject of humility — which he, like all the tradition, regards as foundational. He distinguishes between exterior humility (the performance of humble acts) and interior humility (the genuine conviction of one's need for God). The latter is what he seeks in Philothea. He is characteristically witty about the pathology of false humility: some people are very willing to be thought humble, very eager to be praised for their humility, and deeply offended when others treat them as they have declared themselves to be. Real humility is not distressed by being treated as small; it expects nothing better and is genuinely surprised by any kindness.

“Blessed are the poor in spirit: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the earth.” (Matthew 5:3, 5)

The Beatitudes are Francis's charter for the devout life lived in the world. The poverty of spirit, the meekness, the hunger for righteousness, the mercy — these are not monastic virtues but the character of any soul that has been genuinely converted and is being genuinely formed. They are achievable by the widow managing her household, the merchant conducting his business, the courtier navigating the court, if each does so in dependence on Christ and in love of neighbor.

Francis's treatment of devotion in particular vocations is noteworthy. He addresses the married specifically: marriage is not a concession to weakness but a vocation, and it is sanctified by the same love and fidelity that sanctify the religious life. The married person's path to God runs through the duties of marriage — through faithful love, through the raising of children, through the bearing of each other's faults with patience — not around them. The same logic applies to all states: the particular vocation is not an obstacle to devotion but its primary school.

  • Matthew 5:3–12 — the Beatitudes as the grammar of devotion
  • 1 Corinthians 7:17 — each person abiding in their calling

Perseverance, Relapses, and the Annual Renewal

The fifth and final part of the Introduction addresses perseverance — which Francis regards as the great practical challenge of the devout life. He is not surprised that Philothea will fall. He does not demand that she achieve some threshold of consistency before he considers her genuinely devout. On the contrary, his counsel about relapses is among the most pastorally wise passages in the whole of spiritual literature: when you fall, do not lie there in discouragement and self-pity, do not perform elaborate penances in order to recover your self-esteem, and above all do not use the fall as an argument that devotion is beyond you. Rise quickly, humbly, simply — the way a child rises when it stumbles learning to walk: without drama, with confidence in the hand that is outstretched to help.

Francis is explicitly counter-perfectionist. The person who abandons the devout life because they have failed to maintain it perfectly has confused devotion with self-achievement. Devotion is a gift continuously received, not a standard continuously maintained by will-power. The soul that has fallen and risen fifty times, each time humbly turning back to God, is more genuinely devout — more genuinely dependent on grace — than the soul that has not yet been tested and still trusts in its own consistency.

“I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me.” (Philippians 4:13)

Francis closes with the recommendation of an annual retreat and renewal — a period in which Philothea reviews the state of her soul, her progress and failures in the year past, and renews her acts of consecration and her resolutions. This annual renewal is the temporal structure within which the whole devout life unfolds: not a heroic once-for-all conversion but a patient, recurring return — like the seasons, like the liturgical year, like the daily return to prayer after distraction. Devotion is not a destination reached but a direction sustained, and it is sustained by continual renewal in the love from which it springs.

  • Philippians 4:11 — contentment in every state
  • Philippians 4:13 — strength through Christ for all things
  • John 15:5 — the vine and branches: abiding as the condition of fruit
  • Matthew 5:3–12 — the Beatitudes as the shape of persevering devotion
